relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because population and births move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population leads to more births.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because population and deaths move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population leads to more deaths.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because population and migration move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Migration is calculated as a fraction of the existing population.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because birth fraction and births move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher birth fraction increases the number of births.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because death fraction and deaths move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher death fraction increases the number of deaths.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because base migration and migration move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher base migration fraction increases net migration.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because attractiveness and migration move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Higher housing attractiveness increases net migration into the area.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because attractiveness and migration move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Higher job attractiveness increases net migration into the area.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because availability and attractiveness move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Better housing availability makes the area more attractive for migrants.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because availability and attractiveness move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Better job availability makes the area more attractive for migrants.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because stock and availability move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: More housing units increase the relative availability of housing.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because population and availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population decreases the relative availability of housing per person.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because jobs and availability move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: More jobs increase the relative availability of employment.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because population and availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population decreases the relative availability of jobs per person.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because target and relative availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A higher target for housing per person makes the current availability look lower relatively.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because target and relative availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A higher target for jobs per person makes the current availability look lower relatively.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because stock and construction move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Construction is calculated as a fraction of the existing housing stock.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because stock and demolition move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Demolition is calculated as a fraction of the existing housing stock.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because fraction and construction move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher base construction fraction increases the construction rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because fraction and demolition move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher demolition fraction increases the demolition rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because multiplier and construction move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher construction multiplier increases the construction rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because demand and multiplier move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Higher relative demand for housing encourages more construction.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because population and demand move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population increases the demand for housing.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because stock and demand move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: More housing stock decreases the relative demand for new housing.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because target and relative demand move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A higher target for persons per house makes the current demand look lower relatively.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because structures and starts move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Business starts are calculated as a fraction of the existing business structures.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because structures and closures move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Business closures are calculated as a fraction of the existing business structures.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because fraction and starts move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher base start fraction increases the business start rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because fraction and closures move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher closure fraction increases the business closure rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because multiplier and starts move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A higher start multiplier increases the business start rate.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because availability and multiplier move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: Higher labor availability encourages more business starts.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because population and availability move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: A larger population increases the availability of labor for businesses.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because structures and availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: More business structures decrease the relative availability of labor per business.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Negative polarity because target and relative availability move in opposite directions.
relationships.reasoning: A higher target for labor per business makes the current availability look lower relatively.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because structures and jobs move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: More business structures provide more total jobs.

relationships.polarityReasoning: Positive polarity because jobs per structure and total jobs move in the same direction.
relationships.reasoning: More jobs per structure increase the total number of jobs.

variables.documentation: The number of new residents born per year.
variables.name: Population.births

variables.documentation: The number of residents who die per year.
variables.name: Population.deaths

variables.documentation: The net movement of people into or out of the urban area based on housing and job availability.
variables.name: Population.migration

variables.documentation: The annual birth rate per person.
variables.name: Population.birth fraction

variables.documentation: The annual death rate per person.
variables.name: Population.death fraction

variables.documentation: The normal net migration rate under balanced conditions.
variables.name: Population.base migration fraction

variables.documentation: The desired ratio of housing units per resident.
variables.name: Population.target housing per person

variables.documentation: The desired ratio of jobs per resident.
variables.name: Population.target jobs per person

variables.documentation: The ratio of actual housing per person to the target housing per person.
variables.name: Population.relative housing availability

variables.documentation: The ratio of actual jobs per person to the target jobs per person.
variables.name: Population.relative job availability

variables.documentation: A multiplier representing how housing availability influences migration.
variables.name: Population.attractiveness from housing

variables.documentation: A multiplier representing how job availability influences migration.
variables.name: Population.attractiveness from jobs

variables.documentation: The total number of residents in the urban area.
variables.name: Population.Population

variables.equation: {Enter equation for use when not hooked up to other models}
variables.name: Population.total jobs

variables.documentation: The rate at which new housing units are built.
variables.name: Housing.construction

variables.documentation: The rate at which housing units are removed from the stock.
variables.name: Housing.demolition

variables.documentation: The normal fraction of housing stock added through construction.
variables.name: Housing.base construction fraction

variables.documentation: The fraction of housing stock demolished annually.
variables.name: Housing.demolition fraction

variables.documentation: The desired number of people per housing unit.
variables.name: Housing.target persons per house

variables.documentation: The ratio of actual people per house to the target people per house.
variables.name: Housing.relative housing demand

variables.documentation: A multiplier representing how housing demand influences construction rates.
variables.name: Housing.construction multiplier

variables.documentation: The total number of housing units available.
variables.name: Housing.Housing Stock

variables.documentation: The rate at which new businesses are established.
variables.name: Commerce.business starts

variables.documentation: The rate at which businesses close.
variables.name: Commerce.business closures

variables.documentation: The normal fraction of business structures added through new starts.
variables.name: Commerce.base start fraction

variables.documentation: The fraction of businesses that close annually.
variables.name: Commerce.closure fraction

variables.documentation: The desired number of people available per business structure.
variables.name: Commerce.target labor per business

variables.documentation: The ratio of actual labor availability to the target labor availability.
variables.name: Commerce.relative labor availability

variables.documentation: A multiplier representing how labor availability influences business starts.
variables.name: Commerce.start multiplier

variables.documentation: The average number of jobs provided by each business structure.
variables.name: Commerce.jobs per structure

variables.documentation: The total number of jobs available in the urban area.
variables.name: Commerce.total jobs

variables.documentation: The total number of commercial structures or businesses.
variables.name: Commerce.Business Structures
